questions.question: Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the given word. Admonish
questions.explanation: Explanation: Admonish: This verb means to warn or reprimand someone firmly. It involves expressing disapproval or cautioning against certain actions. Applaud: This verb means to show approval or praise, typically by clapping hands. It conveys a positive reinforcement, which is the opposite of reprimanding or expressing disapproval. Therefore, "applaud" is a suitable antonym for "admonish." The meaning of other words are: Resentment: This noun refers to bitter indignation at having been treated unfairly. It describes a feeling of anger or displeasure about someone or something perceived as wrong or offensive. Benign: This adjective describes something gentle, kind, or harmless. While it has a positive connotation, it doesn't directly oppose the action of admonishing. Dreary: This adjective means dull, bleak, or lifeless. It pertains to the atmosphere or mood and doesn't relate to the act of reprimanding or praising.

questions.question: The following sentence has been divided into parts. One of them may contain an error. Select the part that contains the error from the given options if you don’t find any error, mark ‘No error’ as your answer. Vishnu and his friends / were listening to / the radio all morning.
questions.explanation: Explanation: Since there are no grammatical errors in any part of the sentence, the correct choice is: No error

questions.question: Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom. A blessing in disguise
options: Something that seems bad or unlucky at first, but results in something good happening later.
Taking the first step after somebody's approach
A situation in which no further progress can be made
questions.explanation: Explanation: The idiom "a blessing in disguise" refers to something that initially appears unfortunate or negative but ultimately leads to a positive outcome. Among the given options, the most appropriate meaning is: Something that seems bad or unlucky at first, but results in something good happening later.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the underlined word in the given sentence. I have great admiration and respect for these people; their unselfish act to understand and help people is above and beyond the call of duty.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The word "admiration" refers to a feeling of respect, approval, or high regard for someone or something. Contempt: A noun that signifies a feeling that someone or something is not worthy of respect or approval; disdain. This directly opposes the feeling of admiration. The meanings of other options are: Magnificent: An adjective meaning extremely beautiful, elaborate, or impressive. Scathing: An adjective describing something that is severely critical or scornful. While it has a negative connotation, it does not directly oppose admiration. Cynical: An adjective describing someone who believes that people are motivated purely by self-interest; distrustful of human sincerity or integrity.

questions.question: The following sentence has been split into four segments. Identify the segment that contains a grammatical error. That accident happened /a long time ago, but / I have shuddered at / the thought of it ever for.
options: the thought of it ever for
questions.explanation: Explanation: The segment "the thought of it ever for" contains a grammatical error. The phrase "ever for" is incorrect in this context. The correct expression should be "ever since", which indicates that the action has been ongoing from a specific point in the past until now. Therefore, the corrected sentence is: "That accident happened a long time ago, but I have shuddered at the thought of it ever since."

questions.question: Select the option that can be used as a one-word substitute for the given sentence. Photographers who follow celebrities to get their pictures to sell them
questions.explanation: Explanation: The term "paparazzi" refers to photographers who follow celebrities to capture candid photographs, often to sell them to media outlets. Therefore, the most appropriate one-word substitute for the given description is "paparazzi." The other options are: Reporter: A person who gathers and presents news or information to the public, often working for a newspaper, magazine, television, or online media outlet. Editor: An individual responsible for reviewing, revising, and preparing content (such as articles, books, newspapers, etc.) for publication. Interviewer: A person who conducts interviews, typically to gather information, opinions, or insights from individuals or experts on specific topics.

questions.question: Select the option that expresses the given sentence in indirect speech. He said, “Alas! I am a ruined man.”
options: He applauded with sorrow that he was a ruined man.
He wished that he was a ruined man.
He cried out with sorrow that he was a ruined man.
He confessed with sorrow that he were a ruined man.
questions.explanation: Explanation: In indirect speech, exclamatory expressions like "Alas!" are often replaced with phrases such as "exclaimed with sorrow" to convey the emotion appropriately. Therefore, the option that best expresses the given sentence in indirect speech is: He cried out with sorrow that he was a ruined man. This option accurately reflects the original sentiment and structure of the direct speech.

questions.question: Select the INCORRECTLY spelled word.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The word "Unpretensious" is incorrectly spelled. The correct spelling is "Unpretentious", which describes someone or something that is modest and not attempting to impress others with an appearance of greater importance or value. The other words are spelled correctly: • Grievance: A real or imagined cause for complaint or protest. • Sovereign: A supreme ruler, especially a monarch. • Vicious: Deliberately cruel or violent.

questions.question: Select the option that expresses the given sentence in passive voice. They think that the Governor will resign.
options: They thought that the Governor would be resigning.
It is thought that the Governor will resign.
They think that the Governor is resigning.
It is thought that the Governor will be resigning.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The sentence "They think that the Governor will resign." can be expressed in the passive voice as: "It is thought that the Governor will resign." In this construction, the impersonal phrase "It is thought" is used to convey the general belief or opinion, effectively shifting the focus from the subject "They" to the action itself.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option that can substitute the underlined segment in the given sentence. Doctor wants Tony to cut back down junk foods.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The correct phrase is "to cut back on", which means to reduce the consumption or use of something. Therefore, the sentence should read: "The doctor wants Tony to cut back on junk foods." Here’s a concise summary of the phrasal verbs with "back": • Back away: Move backward due to fear or reluctance. • Back down: Admit defeat or give up a stance. • Back off: Retreat or stop being involved. • Back out: Withdraw from an agreement. • Back up: Support someone, move backward, or create a backup of data. • Back into: Reverse into something, often accidentally. • Back onto: When a property faces something at its rear. • Back out of: Fail to fulfill a promise or withdraw from an arrangement. • Back at: Resume an activity after a break. • Back in: Return to a place or situation. • Back away from: Withdraw from an idea or agreement.

questions.question: Select the option that expresses the given sentence in active voice. Why was such a letter written by your brother?
options: Why your brother wrote such a letter?
Why has your brother written such a letter?
Why is your brother writing such a letter?
Why did your brother write such a letter?
questions.explanation: The correct answer will be: Why did your brother write such a letter? Active voice ----- Passive voice Wh family + did + subject + V1 + object ----- Wh family + was/were +object + V3 + by + subject

questions.question: Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech. She said to me, “Don’t the children like the cake?”
options: she asked me whether the children didn’t like the cake.
She enquired about whether the children liked the cake.
She wondered why the children didn’t like the cake.
She asked me whether the children liked the cake.
questions.explanation: Explanation: In this case: • "Said" is changed to "asked." • The negative question "Don’t the children like the cake?" is transformed into a statement using "whether." • The tense shifts to match the reporting verb's past tense. Answer: She asked me whether the children didn’t like the cake.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blanks. The business operates ________ the same way as the other businesses.
questions.explanation: Explanation: In this context, "much" is used as an adverb to emphasize the similarity between how the business operates and how other businesses operate. This usage aligns with standard English expressions where "much" modifies the degree of similarity. The correct sentence is: "The business operates much the same way as the other businesses."

questions.question: Sentences of a paragraph are given below in jumbled order. Arrange the sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph. (A) I must tell you that I am referring to the tax year which began on 1 April (B) Let me be the first to wish you a very Happy New Year (C) In case none of your friends or family did so (D) Now, before you ask whether the spring sunshine has gone to my head
questions.explanation: Explanation: (C) This sentence sets the context by suggesting that if no one else has extended a particular wish, the speaker is about to do so. (B) Following up, the speaker expresses the wish: "Let me be the first to wish you a very happy New Year." (D) Anticipating potential confusion about the timing of this New Year greeting, the speaker preempts with: "Now, before you ask whether the spring sunshine has gone to my head." (A) Finally, the speaker clarifies the reference: "I must tell you that I am referring to the tax year which began on 1 April." The answer is CBDA.

questions.question: Select the option that can be used as a one-word substitute for the given sentence. The art of identifying a disease from its signs and symptoms.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The most appropriate one-word substitute for "the art of identifying a disease from its signs and symptoms" is diagnosis. The other options are: Biopsy: A medical procedure involving the extraction of a sample of tissue for examination to determine the presence or extent of a disease. Prognosis: A forecast of the likely course or outcome of a disease, indicating the chances of recovery. Autopsy: A post-mortem examination of a body to determine the cause of death.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom. Costs an arm and a leg
questions.explanation: Explanation: The idiom "costs an arm and a leg" means something is extremely expensive. This expression suggests that the price of an item is so high that it feels as though one would have to sacrifice something as valuable as an arm and a leg to afford it.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate synonym of the given word. Procrastination
questions.explanation: Explanation: Procrastination refers to the act of delaying or postponing tasks or decisions. Suspension involves a temporary halt or delay in an activity or process, aligning with the concept of postponement inherent in procrastination. The meaning of other options are: Determined: Indicates a firm decision or resolve, which is the opposite of procrastination. Elevated: Means raised or increased, unrelated to the concept of delay. Dignified: Describes a state of being worthy or honorable, not connected to postponement.

questions.question: Parts of a sentence are given below in jumbled order. Arrange the parts in the correct order to form a meaningful sentence. O) arid deserts P) from lush forests to Q) hikers encountered diverse ecosystems R) trekking through the wilderness
questions.explanation: Explanation: (R) "trekking through the wilderness" This phrase sets the context by describing the action of trekking. (Q) "hikers encountered diverse ecosystems" This follows logically, indicating what hikers experienced during their trek. (P) "from lush forests to" This begins to specify the range of ecosystems encountered, starting with lush forests. (O) "arid deserts" This completes the range, contrasting lush forests with arid deserts. The correct arrangement of the sentence parts is RQPO.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. You can tell yourself that you would be willing to lose everything you have in order to get something you want: all of those things you’re willing to lose are what make you___________.
questions.explanation: Explanation: In this context, the sentence suggests that the possessions or attributes you're willing to sacrifice are integral to your identity—they make you recognizable. The most appropriate option to fill in the blank is recognizable. The other options are: Distract: Means to divert attention, which doesn't fit the context of defining identity. Pessimist: Refers to someone who expects the worst, unrelated to the concept of identity in this sentence. Unfortunately: An adverb expressing regret, which doesn't logically complete the sentence.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate synonym of the given word. Constraints
questions.explanation: Explanation: Constraints refer to limitations or restrictions that control what can be done. Restrictions similarly denote limitations imposed on someone or something, making it a suitable synonym. The meanings of other options are: Ambiguous means unclear or open to multiple interpretations, which doesn't align with the meaning of constraints. Murky describes something that is dark or unclear, unrelated to the concept of limitations. Distinguished refers to someone who is respected and eminent, not connected to the idea of constraints.

questions.question: Fill in the blanks with the most appropriate words to complete the passage. For each blank, select the most appropriate word from the given option. What are the future possibilities for space travel in the long term? (1) _____ will humans go after they have explored our solar system? It is said that ‘Star Trek’ could hold the (2) _____ to these questions. It is predicted that travel to other galaxies will only be (3) _____ in huge ‘starships’. These will wander through space for hundreds or even thousands of years. Each of them will carry a large human (4) _____. Some of those people will take over a suitable new planet and settle down there. This search for new planets to settle in will continue until life on planet Earth could be just a (5) ______ memory. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank 1.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The question seeks to identify the next destinations for human exploration beyond our solar system. "Where" inquiries about a place or location, fitting the context of seeking future destinations.

questions.question: Fill in the blanks with the most appropriate words to complete the passage. For each blank, select the most appropriate word from the given option. What are the future possibilities for space travel in the long term? (1) _____ will humans go after they have explored our solar system? It is said that ‘Star Trek’ could hold the (2) _____ to these questions. It is predicted that travel to other galaxies will only be (3) _____ in huge ‘starships’. These will wander through space for hundreds or even thousands of years. Each of them will carry a large human (4) _____. Some of those people will take over a suitable new planet and settle down there. This search for new planets to settle in will continue until life on planet Earth could be just a (5) ______ memory. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank 2.
questions.explanation: Explanation: Answer: Fits the context as it refers to a solution or explanation to the questions posed about future space travel. "It is said that 'Star Trek' could hold the answer to these questions."

questions.question: Fill in the blanks with the most appropriate words to complete the passage. For each blank, select the most appropriate word from the given option. What are the future possibilities for space travel in the long term? (1) _____ will humans go after they have explored our solar system? It is said that ‘Star Trek’ could hold the (2) _____ to these questions. It is predicted that travel to other galaxies will only be (3) _____ in huge ‘starships’. These will wander through space for hundreds or even thousands of years. Each of them will carry a large human (4) _____. Some of those people will take over a suitable new planet and settle down there. This search for new planets to settle in will continue until life on planet Earth could be just a (5) ______ memory. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank 3.
questions.explanation: Explanation: Possible: Indicates that such travel can occur under certain conditions, fitting the context of future predictions about intergalactic travel. "It is predicted that travel to other galaxies will only be possible in huge 'starships'."

questions.question: Fill in the blanks with the most appropriate words to complete the passage. For each blank, select the most appropriate word from the given option. What are the future possibilities for space travel in the long term? (1) _____ will humans go after they have explored our solar system? It is said that ‘Star Trek’ could hold the (2) _____ to these questions. It is predicted that travel to other galaxies will only be (3) _____ in huge ‘starships’. These will wander through space for hundreds or even thousands of years. Each of them will carry a large human (4) _____. Some of those people will take over a suitable new planet and settle down there. This search for new planets to settle in will continue until life on planet Earth could be just a (5) ______ memory. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank 4.
questions.explanation: Explanation: Population: Refers to a group of individuals of the same species inhabiting a particular area. In this context, it denotes the large number of humans that the starships will carry. "Each of them will carry a large human population."

questions.question: Fill in the blanks with the most appropriate words to complete the passage. For each blank, select the most appropriate word from the given option. What are the future possibilities for space travel in the long term? (1) _____ will humans go after they have explored our solar system? It is said that ‘Star Trek’ could hold the (2) _____ to these questions. It is predicted that travel to other galaxies will only be (3) _____ in huge ‘starships’. These will wander through space for hundreds or even thousands of years. Each of them will carry a large human (4) _____. Some of those people will take over a suitable new planet and settle down there. This search for new planets to settle in will continue until life on planet Earth could be just a (5) ______ memory. Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank 5.
questions.explanation: Explanation: Distant: In this context, "distant memory" is a common expression meaning something remembered faintly from the past. It implies that life on Earth has become a faint recollection due to the passage of time. "This search for new planets to settle in will continue until life on planet Earth could be just a distant memory."
